1: The second Levitical census of Numbers focuses on the sub-division of labor within the Levitical tribe. Chapter 3 counted all Levites from one month old; chapter 4 counts those in the prime service range (30-50 years) by sub-clan — Kohath, Gershon, Merarite — and assigns each clan its specific tabernacle-carrying duty. This organizational census is the practical implementation of Lev 8 ordination: the ordained priesthood manages the holy objects; the Levitical guilds carry them.
2: nasa et rosh beney Qehat: 'Take a census [lit. lift the head] of the Kohathites from among the Levites.' The Kohathites are named first because they carry the most holy objects (the ark, table, lampstand, altars). They are the most restricted in access — they cannot touch or look at the objects directly — yet they bear the most sacred burden. This inversion (greatest honor = greatest restriction + greatest danger) runs through all of Numbers 4's holiness theology.
3: misheloshim shanah vamalah ve'ad chamishshim shanah: 'from thirty years old and upward to fifty years old.' The age range for active Levitical service is 30-50, the prime years of physical strength. Numbers 8:24-25 gives a slightly different range (25-50) for the broader Levitical service, which may distinguish training years (25-30) from full active service. After 50 the Levite assists but no longer carries (avodah = service/work, cognate to the general labor term).
4: zot avodat beney Qehat be'ohel mo'ed qodesh haqqodashim: 'This is the work assigned to the Kohathites in the tent of meeting: the care of the most holy objects.' Qodesh haqqodashim (holy of holies / most holy things) — the superlative construction: the holiest of holy things. The Kohathites carry what even ordinary Israelites cannot see or touch, what even the priests approach with elaborate precaution. Their carrying responsibility is the most dangerous and the most honored of all Levitical work.
5: vebah Aharon uvanav: 'Aaron and his sons shall go in' — the priests enter first to wrap before the Kohathites approach. The covering protocol (described in v5-14) is exhaustive: the ark gets the veil, then a leather cover, then blue cloth; every object is wrapped in multiple layers before the Kohathites lift it. The sequence establishes a key theological principle: holy things require priestly mediation even when they are being moved. No Levite may approach the uncovered holy things.
6: The ark's outer covering is or tachash — translated variously as 'porpoise/dugong skin,' 'fine leather,' or 'badger skin.' The exact identification is uncertain; the Targumim translates it as the skin of a particular sea creature (sasgona). What is clear is that it is a durable, weather-resistant outer covering — the most opaque outer layer. The blue cloth placed on top (v6b, the beged techelet) identifies the object as belonging to the sanctuary. The double covering (opaque leather + identifying color) keeps both hidden and marked.
7: The table of the Presence (shulchan hapanim, lit. 'table of the face/presence') receives a blue cloth and all its utensils — the dishes (qe'arot), the pans (kappot), the jars (menaqiyyot), and the bowls (qesavot) used in the tamid bread ritual. The table held twelve loaves of bread representing the twelve tribes before YHWH continually (Leviticus 24:5-8). The detail of wrapping all the utensils individually mirrors the care given to the ark — every object associated with YHWH's presence receives proportionate reverence.
8: A scarlet cloth over the table's blue cloth, then the leather outer cover. The color sequence — blue (identity/holiness), scarlet (life/blood?), leather (protection) — has been variously interpreted, but the practical point is layering for both concealment and protection during wilderness transit. The NT sees the wrapping of holy things as the type of what was hidden in the OT and revealed in Christ: 'the mystery hidden for ages' (Colossians 1:26) is the concealed-then-revealed logic that the veiled ark enacts.
9: The lampstand (menorah) and all its utensils — the lamp-cups (neroteiha), the snuffers (malqacheiha), the trays (machtoteiha) — wrapped in blue cloth. The menorah is the sanctuary's light-source, the seven-branched lamp that burns perpetually (Exodus 27:20). Unlike the ark, it has no poles — it is placed on a carrying frame (mot, v10). The care given to the lampstand during transit mirrors its unique function: it must arrive intact to resume its burning, the light of YHWH's presence.
10: venatenuh al hamot: 'they shall put it on the carrying frame.' The word mot appears only here and in v12 for carrying frames/bars used with objects that have no poles. The verb natan al (place upon) rather than the usual pole-insertion language suggests a different carrying mechanism — perhaps a stretcher-like arrangement. The precise technology is unclear; the theological point is that every holy object has a designated mode of transport, and none is dragged or left to improvisation.
11: The gold incense altar (mizbach hazahav) — the small altar inside the tent on which incense is burned daily and on which Yom Kippur blood is applied (Leviticus 4:7; Numbers 16:18-19). It receives only a blue cloth and leather cover. Notably, it does not get the scarlet middle cloth that the table gets. Each object's covering is customized to its function and position within the sanctuary hierarchy.
12: ucheley hasharet asher yeshartyu baqodesh: 'all the utensils of service used in the sanctuary.' The service utensils — everything used in the daily and festival rituals — are wrapped in blue cloth, covered in leather, and placed on a carrying frame. The word sharet (serve/minister) is the verb used for Levitical and priestly service throughout the OT, and its noun form mesharetim applies to both humans and the objects they use. The utensils themselves participate in the ministry.
13: The altar of burnt offering (mizbach ha'olah) — the large bronze altar — is cleared of ashes (dishenu, from deshen = fat/ashes of sacrifices) and covered with purple cloth (beged argaman). The purple cloth is unique to this altar among all the covered objects. Purple (argaman) is the color of kingship and royalty in the ancient world (Proverbs 31:22; Judges 8:26). The altar of sacrifice — where YHWH's judgment of sin is enacted — is covered in the color of royal authority.
14: All the utensils of the altar — the fire pans (hamachtot), the forks (hamizlagot), the shovels (hayya'im), the basins (hamizraqot) — placed on the altar and covered with leather. These are the working implements of the sacrificial system: the tools that handle burning flesh, blood, and ash. They are treated with the same care as the sanctuary's decorative furnishings — all service objects, functional or ornamental, require appropriate reverence when carried through the wilderness.
15: velo yig'u el haqodesh vametu: 'they shall not touch the holy things, lest they die.' The death-warning for unauthorized touching of holy objects echoes Uzzah's death at the threshing floor of Nacon (2 Samuel 6:6-7) — he reached out to steady the ark and died. The holiness-danger is not incidental to the tabernacle system but central to it: YHWH's presence has a power that destroys the unprotected, the unauthorized, or the disrespectful. The carrying instructions exist because holy things can kill, not merely because they are valuable.
16: Eleazar ben Aharon hakohen: Eleazar son of Aaron is given supervisory oversight over the Kohathite clan's work and is personally responsible for the oil, the incense, the tamid grain offering, and the anointing oil. His role is that of the chief liaison between the high priestly family and the clan that carries the most dangerous objects. When Aaron eventually dies (Numbers 20:28), Eleazar becomes high priest — his administrative training in Numbers 4 prepares him for this succession.
17: A second divine address (v17-20) focuses specifically on the danger to the Kohathites themselves. YHWH's concern is not only for the integrity of the holy objects but for the preservation of the Kohathite clan. The imperative 'do not let the Kohathite clan be cut off (yikarot) from among the Levites' (v18) reflects YHWH's pastoral concern: the protocol exists to protect those who carry the holy things, not merely to protect the holy things.
18: al takhritu et shevet Qehat: 'Do not cut off the clan of the Kohathites.' The verb karat (cut off) in this context means annihilation through divine judgment — the same term used for covenant-cutting and for the punishment of covenant-breakers (Leviticus 7:20-21). The Kohathites are at greatest risk of this judgment because they are closest to the holy things. YHWH warns Moses and Aaron not to allow circumstances to develop that would trigger this judgment — the protective protocol is not bureaucracy but pastoral care.
19: ze'u lahem: 'but do this for them' — Aaron and his sons are to assign each Kohathite his specific object and role before the camp moves. The personal assignment prevents two dangers: (1) confusion during transit that might lead to unauthorized contact; (2) curiosity that might lead to unauthorized looking. The phrase 'so that they may live and not die' (vechayyu velo yamuthu) is remarkable — it is addressed to the priests as a responsibility toward their subordinates. The priest's duty includes protecting those in his care from inadvertent death.
20: velo yavo'u lirot kebala et haqodesh vametu: 'the Kohathites themselves must not go in to look at the holy things even for a moment, or they will die.' The phrase kebala' means 'for a moment / in the act of swallowing' — a colloquial expression for the briefest instant. Even a momentary glance at the uncovered holy things brings death. This is not metaphorical: the Kohathites' restriction is total and absolute. The principle that drives the entire chapter is that YHWH's holiness is not safe for the unconsecrated, regardless of how briefly the exposure occurs.
21: YHWH addresses Moses a third time (v21-28) for the Gershonite assignments. The structural repetition — divine address + census description + work assignment + supervisory assignment — establishes the organizational pattern for all three Levitical clans. The Gershonites carry the curtains and coverings (the textile elements of the tabernacle), a lighter but substantial load given the size of the curtain system (Exod 26).
22: nasa et rosh beney Gershon: The Gershonites are the second clan counted. They are descendants of Gershon, Levi's firstborn (Genesis 46:11), yet they receive a secondary assignment — not the most holy objects but the curtains and coverings. Birth order does not determine holiness rank in Israel; YHWH's assignment determines function. The Kohathites, though second-born (through Kohath, Levi's second son), carry the holiest objects.
23: The Gershonites also serve from age 30-50. The same tsava (service/host) language used in v3 appears here — tzava also describes the military host, and both military service and sanctuary service share the term. The Levites are YHWH's army, organized, age-stratified, and commanded, though their weapons are curtains and poles.
24: zot avodat mishpechot haGershuni la'avod ulmassa: 'service and carrying.' The two words 'avodah (service/work) and massa (burden/load to be carried) define the Gershonites' dual role: they perform the service of caring for the fabric elements and bear the physical load of carrying them. The distinction between 'avodah and massa will recur as a legal category in later Levitical regulation.
25: The Gershonites carry: the curtains of the tabernacle (yeri'ot hamishkan), the tent of meeting with its covering, the covering of tachash leather over it, the screen for the door of the tent, the courtyard hangings. This is the largest textile load — multiple layers of large curtains that form the walls, roof, and courtyard enclosure of the tabernacle complex. Without the Gershonite load, the tabernacle cannot be erected at the next campsite.
26: The courtyard screen (masak sha'ar hechatser) and the cords (meytarim) that secure the courtyard — the Gershonites carry everything woven or fabricated for the tabernacle's external structure. The meticulous enumeration of each textile element mirrors the construction specifications of Exodus 26-27, creating a literary correspondence between the construction account and the carrying account.
27: al pi Aharon uvanav tihyeh kol avodat beney haGershuni: 'under the direction of Aaron and his sons shall all the work of the Gershonites be done.' The Gershonites are not self-supervising — all their service (carrying, erecting, dismantling) is done under Aaronic direction. This chain of command mirrors the broader structure: YHWH → Moses → Aaron and sons → Levitical clans. The Levites serve the priesthood; the priesthood serves YHWH.
28: vetachat yad Ithamar ben Aharon hakohen: Ithamar, Aaron's fourth and youngest son, supervises the Gershonites (as he also supervises the Merarites in v33). The assignment of the two younger Aaron's sons (Eleazar to Kohath, Ithamar to Gershon and Merarite) distributes supervisory responsibility across the priestly family. After Nadab and Abihu's deaths (Lev 10), only Eleazar and Ithamar remain to carry the priestly line.
29: The Merarites receive the third census and assignment. They carry the structural-load elements: frames (qershim), crossbars (berichim), pillars ('ammudim), bases ('adenim), pegs (yetedot), ropes (meytarim). The Merarite load is the heaviest in physical weight — the bronze and acacia-wood frame elements — though it lacks the holiness-danger of the Kohathite load. Their census result (3,200, v44) is the largest of the three clans, appropriate given their heavier physical burden.
30: The Merarites begin their service at the same 30-50 year range. The repetition of the age-range across all three clans (v3, v23, v30) establishes it as the universal standard for full Levitical service, not a clan-specific rule. The age range may reflect the physical demands of tabernacle carrying — thirty is mature enough for responsibility; fifty is when the physical strength for heavy carrying begins to decline.
31: vezeh mishmeret massa'am: 'this is what they are responsible to carry as their entire service.' The Merarites are given itemized responsibility for the structural elements: the frames (qershim), the crossbars (berichim), the pillars and their bases for the tabernacle and courtyard. The level of specification — every peg, every rope — establishes individual accountability for each component. The tabernacle is a complex structure and each piece has a guardian.
32: The pegs (yetedot) and ropes (meytarim) — the smallest and least glamorous components — are also itemized and assigned to the Merarites. The thoroughness of the inventory extends to what might seem like insignificant hardware. No component of the tabernacle is beneath the level of named responsibility. The principle: everything that enables YHWH's dwelling to stand is important, regardless of its visibility or perceived significance.
33: vetachat yad Ithamar ben Aharon hakohen: Ithamar supervises both Gershon and Merarite — the two clans that carry the less-holy but still essential structural and textile elements. Eleazar supervises the Kohathites who carry the most holy objects. The distribution of oversight reflects the hierarchy of holiness: the higher the holiness-risk, the higher the supervisory rank required.
34: The formal census results begin (v34-49). Moses, Aaron, and the leaders count each clan systematically. The verb pakad (muster/number/assign) appears repeatedly throughout Numbers 4 — it carries both the sense of counting and of appointing/assigning. The census is not merely a headcount but a commissioning: each counted Levite is assigned a specific burden and held accountable for it.
35: The age qualification formula is restated at the census results: 'all from thirty to fifty years old who qualified to serve.' The repetition between the command (v3) and the census-result (v35) confirms that the command was executed faithfully and completely. This correspondence between divine command and faithful execution is a structural feature of both the Exodus construction narrative and the Numbers census narratives.
36: Kohathite count: alpayim sheva me'ot vachamishshim — 2,750. The specific number mirrors the precision of the tabernacle specifications. These are not round estimates but precise records of the working-age population of the Kohathite sub-clan at a specific historical moment (the second year after the Exodus).
37: The summary formula: 'These are those enrolled from the Kohathite clans — all who served (kol ha'oved avodah) in the tent of meeting — whom Moses and Aaron enrolled.' The double attestation (Moses and Aaron together) and the reference to YHWH's command through Moses creates a chain of authority: YHWH → Moses → Aaron → census → Kohathites. The Levitical organization is divinely ordered at every level.
38: Gershonite census begins. The same formula structure (by clans, ancestral houses, age range) is applied to each sub-clan, creating a meticulous parallel structure. The literary pattern enforces the organizational equality of the three clans in terms of procedural rigor, even though their specific assignments differ in holiness-proximity.
39: The qualifying phrase restated for Gershon: 'all from thirty to fifty years old who qualified to work.' The Hebrew qal-haba' litsvo' (everyone who enters to serve, lit. enters to the host) suggests something like 'all who are enrolled into the service-army' — the Levites are YHWH's holy army of sanctuary workers, organized like a military unit.
40: Gershonite count: alpayim shesh me'ot usheloshim — 2,630. Slightly less than the Kohathites (2,750), consistent with the typical Kohathite demographic dominance in Numbers' Levitical censuses. The three numbers together (Kohath 2,750 + Gershon 2,630 + Merarite 3,200 = 8,580) will be confirmed in v48.
41: Summary for Gershon: same double-attestation formula as v37. The consistent structure across the three clan summaries creates a literary triptych — each clan is treated with identical procedural honor, regardless of the relative holiness of its assigned objects.
42: Merarite census introduction. The Merarites, carrying the structural load, are the largest sub-clan (3,200 in v44), suggesting that YHWH's organization of the Levites accounted for the physical demands of each carrying assignment by allocating appropriate workforce.
43: The qualifying age range formula restated a third time — its repetition across all three sub-clan censuses is itself a literary statement of thoroughness and order. The God who organized the tabernacle's construction with precise specifications organizes its transportation with the same precision.
44: Merarite count: sheloshet alafim umayim — 3,200. The largest of the three sub-clans, carrying the heaviest physical load. The organizational wisdom visible here is that YHWH assigned the most structurally essential (and heaviest) load to the most numerous sub-clan — a practical matching of workforce to workload.
45: Summary for Merarite: the standard attestation formula. Three sub-clans, three censuses, three summary formulas — the entire chapter has the character of an official military register. The tabernacle is YHWH's mishkan (dwelling) and ohel mo'ed (tent of meeting), and it moves through the wilderness supported by a precisely organized army of carriers.
46: The total Levitical active-service count is introduced (v46-49): 'All the Levites whom Moses, Aaron, and the leaders of Israel enrolled.' The three leadership levels (Moses = prophet/lawgiver; Aaron = high priest; leaders = tribal elders) are all involved in the final census — this is the most authoritative accounting possible within the Israelite organizational structure.
47: mikol mela'khet ha'avodah uma'aseh hamassa: 'everyone who could qualify to perform the service work and the carrying work.' The two categories — avodah (service/ministry) and massa (carrying/burden) — capture the double nature of Levitical work: the skilled service of managing, assembling, and caring for the sanctuary components, plus the physical labor of carrying them through the wilderness. Both are sacred; neither is merely mechanical.
48: Total: shemonat alafim vachamesh me'ot ushmonim — 8,580. This is the sum of 2,750 + 2,630 + 3,200. The precision of the total (not rounded) and its arithmetic correctness are marks of an authentic census record. The 8,580 active-service Levites represent the organizational core of Israel's wilderness sanctuary operation.
49: ish ish al avodato ve'al massa'o: 'each was assigned to his service and his load.' The final summary is personal and specific — not a mass assignment but individual accountability. Each Levite had a named role and a named burden. YHWH's organization extends to the individual level: the God who knows the number of the stars (Psalms 147:4) also assigns each Levite his specific carrying responsibility by name.
